title: AESL Appointed Consultants for GFA Technical Centre Accommodation Project
excerpt: The Ghana Football Association has appointed Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) as consultants for the development of a 40-bed accommodation facility at the GFA Technical Centre in Prampram.
content:
The Ghana Football Association (GFA) has appointed Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) as the consulting firm responsible for the construction of a new 40-bed accommodation facility at the GFA Technical Centre in Prampram.

Under the consultancy agreement, AESL will provide professional services across the full project lifecycle, including pre-contract activities, technical reviews, procurement support, contract administration, construction supervision, quality control, cost monitoring, and final project handover.

The project is aimed at creating a modern accommodation facility that meets international standards while improving the operational capacity of the Technical Centre. The facility will support Ghana’s national teams, technical programmes, and visiting officials who use the centre.

The partnership between the GFA and AESL forms part of ongoing efforts to strengthen sports infrastructure in Ghana and provide improved facilities that support the long-term growth and development of football.

title: Seven-Member AESL Board Inaugurated to Strengthen Company Growth
excerpt: A newly constituted board of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been inaugurated with a mandate to provide strategic leadership and explore opportunities for growth beyond Ghana.
content:
A seven-member board of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been officially inaugurated in Accra with a charge to provide strong leadership and develop strategies that will position the company for greater impact within Ghana and the West African region.

The Minister of Works and Housing, Francis Asenso-Boakye, who administered the oath of office and secrecy, encouraged the board members to guide AESL towards becoming a stronger player in the construction and consultancy industry.

The Minister highlighted opportunities for AESL to expand its services beyond Ghana, particularly into neighbouring countries such as Burkina Faso, Mali, and Niger, where professional engineering and consultancy expertise are needed.

He also advised the board to focus on creating partnerships with private sector organisations rather than depending solely on government projects, noting that private companies remain an important part of the construction industry's growth.

Mr Asenso-Boakye reminded the board that its responsibility was to provide strategic direction, supervise management performance, establish standards, and ensure accountability rather than interfere in the daily administration of the company.

The Chairperson of the board, Professor Kwame Asamoah, expressed appreciation for the confidence placed in the board and pledged their commitment to improving AESL through professionalism, integrity, value for money, and effective leadership.

title: AESL Board Inaugurated to Explore New Revenue Opportunities and Strategic Growth
excerpt: The newly inaugurated board of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been tasked to expand the company's revenue base, strengthen partnerships, and position the institution for sustainable growth.
content:
An eight-member governing board of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been inaugurated with a mandate to explore new opportunities that will strengthen the company's financial position and improve its role as a leading public sector consultancy institution.

The Minister of Works, Housing and Water Resources, Kenneth Gilbert Adjei, charged the board to develop strategies aimed at increasing AESL's revenue through stronger engagement with ministries, departments, agencies, Metropolitan, Municipal and District Assemblies (MMDAs), and other organisations requiring professional consultancy services.

The Minister acknowledged AESL's reputation for professionalism and technical expertise, encouraging the board to leverage these strengths through strategic investments, partnerships, improved client relationships, and expansion into emerging sectors including roads, oil and gas, and infrastructure development.

The board was also informed about ongoing discussions regarding a possible realignment or merger between AESL and the Public Works Department (PWD). The Minister urged the board to carefully assess the proposal and provide recommendations based on the potential benefits and challenges.

Mr Adjei commended AESL management for resolving outstanding obligations owed to institutions including SSNIT, the Ghana Revenue Authority (GRA), and retired staff members.

The Board Chairman, Mr Kasser Ambrose Tee, expressed gratitude for the opportunity to serve and stated that the board would review the legal framework establishing AESL while developing strategies that align the institution with modern trends in architectural, engineering, and consultancy services.

The board pledged to work towards repositioning AESL as a stronger and more competitive organisation capable of delivering quality professional services for national development.

Source: https://gna.org.gh/2025/07/aesl-board-inaugurated-charged-to-explore-avenues-to-boost-revenue/

title: Bank of Ghana Engages AESL for Value-for-Money Audit of New Headquarters Project
excerpt: The Bank of Ghana has announced plans to engage Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to conduct a value-for-money audit of its new headquarters project to promote transparency and accountability.
content:
The Bank of Ghana (BoG) has announced plans to engage Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to undertake a value-for-money audit of its new headquarters project.

The decision follows public discussions and concerns surrounding the cost of the new Bank of Ghana headquarters facility, with the central bank seeking an independent assessment to provide clarity on the project's financial efficiency.

Speaking before Parliament, the Governor of the Bank of Ghana stated that the audit would form part of measures aimed at improving transparency, accountability, and public confidence in the execution of major infrastructure projects.

AESL, a state-owned architectural and engineering consultancy institution, is expected to assess whether the project cost reflects prevailing market conditions and provide recommendations on opportunities for improving value and cost efficiency.

The audit is also expected to support the Bank of Ghana's commitment to prudent financial management and responsible use of public resources.

The central bank emphasised that transparency, sound decision-making, and accountability remain essential principles guiding its operations and efforts to maintain public trust.

title: AESL Tasked to Conduct Structural Integrity Assessment of Accra International Conference Centre
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been directed to conduct a structural integrity assessment of the Accra International Conference Centre to determine the safety and suitability of the facility.
content:
The Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been tasked to undertake a structural integrity assessment of the Accra International Conference Centre (AICC) following concerns about the condition of the facility.

The directive was issued by the Minister of Foreign Affairs, Samuel Okudzeto Ablakwa, who requested that AESL provide a comprehensive structural report within three months to determine whether the facility remains safe and fit for purpose.

The assessment follows reports of structural defects at the conference centre, raising concerns about the safety of users and the continued hosting of major national and international events at the facility.

The Accra International Conference Centre, which was constructed in 1991, remains one of Ghana's most important venues for conferences, diplomatic engagements, seminars, and other major gatherings.

The facility includes several conference halls, committee rooms, and support spaces designed to accommodate large numbers of delegates during local and international events.

The structural evaluation by AESL is expected to provide an expert assessment of the building's current condition and recommend any required maintenance, rehabilitation, or improvement works.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/1389482/ablakwa-directs-aesl-to-present-structural-integri.html

title: AESL Board Inaugurated to Drive Strategic Growth and Infrastructure Development
excerpt: A new eight-member board of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been inaugurated with a mandate to provide strategic leadership, improve service delivery, and reposition the company as a key player in Ghana’s infrastructure development.
content:
The eight-member Board of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been officially inaugurated with a mandate to provide strategic oversight and reposition the organisation as a leading institution in Ghana’s infrastructure development sector.

The inauguration was performed by the Minister for Works, Housing and Water Resources, Mr Kenneth Gilbert Adjei, who charged the board members to provide effective leadership, strengthen operations, and support AESL’s transformation agenda.

The board, chaired by Mr Kasser Ambrose Tee, is made up of professionals from diverse fields including architecture, engineering, law, finance, and quantity surveying. Other members include Mr Kofi Adjei Baafi, Mr Worlase Kpeli, Mr Gordon Tanga, Mr Peter Abrefa Damoah (Osagyefo Ampem Amoapong Tabrako III), Madam Irene Messiba, Mr Badombie Bakuoro Fuseini, and Mr Yanore George Aduko.

Speaking at the ceremony, the Minister emphasised the important role AESL plays in Ghana’s built environment and urged the board to work closely with management to address operational challenges, improve efficiency, and enhance the company’s competitiveness.

He also encouraged the board to explore innovative approaches to increase revenue, establish stronger partnerships with stakeholders, and expand AESL’s role in delivering sustainable and climate-conscious infrastructure solutions.

The Minister further advised the board to carefully assess the proposed realignment of AESL with the Public Works Department (PWD), ensuring that any decision is guided by proper analysis and engagement with relevant stakeholders.

In response, the Board Chairman, Mr Kasser Ambrose Tee, expressed appreciation for the opportunity to serve and pledged the board’s commitment to transparency, institutional reforms, and improving AESL’s impact.

He stated that the board would review AESL’s operational framework, address existing challenges, and develop strategies to position the company as a more efficient and solutions-driven organisation capable of serving both public and private sector clients.

The board reaffirmed its commitment to strengthening AESL’s relevance in Ghana’s infrastructure development through professionalism, innovation, and quality consultancy services.

title: AESL Appeals for Greater Share of Government Consultancy Projects
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has appealed for a larger share of government consultancy projects to strengthen its financial position, improve productivity, and maximise its technical expertise.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has appealed to government to allocate a greater percentage of public sector consultancy projects to the company to enable it to become financially stronger and more effective in delivering professional services.

The appeal was made by the Board Chairman of AESL, Professor Kwame Asamoah, during the company’s Annual General Meeting, where the organisation presented its annual reports and financial statements.

Professor Asamoah explained that AESL possesses significant technical expertise that can contribute to Ghana’s infrastructure development agenda. He noted that providing the company with more opportunities would allow it to utilise its professional capacity while supporting national development initiatives.

He stated that AESL must explore innovative ways of generating revenue, including expanding its services in areas such as building assessments, structural integrity evaluations, and other professional consultancy services instead of relying mainly on government support.

The Board Chairman encouraged staff and management to uphold professionalism, discipline, integrity, and ethical standards as part of efforts to build a stronger and more competitive institution.

The Managing Director of AESL, Mr Isaac Agyei Marfo, highlighted some of the challenges facing the company, including limited access to major consultancy opportunities despite submitting technical and financial proposals for several projects.

He explained that while several government projects were undertaken during the period under review, AESL was not engaged as consultants on many of them, affecting the company’s revenue generation capacity.

Mr Marfo added that AESL would continue to pursue opportunities within Ghana and other African markets while strengthening internal systems to improve service delivery.

The Minister for Works and Housing, Mr Francis Asenso-Boakye, acknowledged AESL’s contribution to Ghana’s development and encouraged public institutions to take advantage of the company’s expertise in architecture, engineering, surveying, and consultancy services.

He urged AESL to remain innovative, competitive, and proactive in attracting new clients while assuring that steps would be taken to support the company’s growth.

title: AESL Calls for Increased Government Support Through Public Sector Projects
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has called for increased participation in public sector projects, highlighting its capacity to deliver professional architecture, engineering, surveying, and consultancy services across Ghana.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has called on government to increase its engagement with the company in the execution of public sector projects, noting that the organisation has developed the expertise required to manage major infrastructure assignments.

The Board Chairman of AESL, Dr Kwame Asamoah, stated that the company had repositioned itself with renewed professional capacity to undertake significant projects within Ghana’s built environment.

Speaking during a ceremony to commission new operational vehicles acquired by AESL to improve project monitoring activities across its regional offices, Dr Asamoah explained that the company had implemented measures aimed at strengthening its operations and improving efficiency.

He said AESL remained committed to supporting Ghana’s infrastructure development goals and would continue providing strategic leadership, professional consultancy, and technical expertise in the construction sector.

The Managing Director of AESL, Mr Isaac Agyei Marfo, noted that the company had extensive capabilities but needed greater awareness among public institutions regarding the range of services it provides.

He explained that AESL offers professional services covering architecture, civil and structural engineering, electrical and mechanical engineering, land surveying, quantity surveying, project supervision, and construction quality control.

The Minister for Works and Housing, Mr Atta Akyea, expressed confidence that the newly acquired vehicles would enhance AESL’s ability to supervise projects effectively, especially across the regions.

He assured that efforts would be made to provide opportunities for AESL to participate in more government projects while encouraging the organisation to continue improving its competitiveness.

AESL was established as a professional consultancy institution under the Ministry of Works and Housing to provide architectural, engineering, and surveying services to government agencies, private organisations, and individuals.

Over the years, the company has contributed to several major public infrastructure projects, including hospital facilities, stadium rehabilitation works, district assembly infrastructure, and engineering supervision services.

title: AESL Addresses Debt Challenges Through Improved Contract Management and Recovery Measures
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has introduced measures to recover outstanding debts and strengthen its financial position by ensuring proper contractual agreements with clients before undertaking projects.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has acknowledged that the absence of formal contractual agreements with some clients contributed to the accumulation of outstanding debts owed to the organisation over the years.

The Managing Director of AESL, Mr Isaac Adjei Marfo, explained that the company had implemented new measures to improve debt recovery and ensure that future projects are executed under clear contractual arrangements.

Appearing before the Public Accounts Committee (PAC) of Parliament, Mr Marfo stated that AESL had adopted project-specific contracts with clients before commencing any consultancy work. This approach, he noted, would provide the necessary documentation to support claims and facilitate the recovery of fees for services rendered.

The move follows concerns raised in the Auditor General’s report regarding AESL’s outstanding debt portfolio and the need for stronger mechanisms to recover payments owed by government agencies and other clients.

Mr Marfo indicated that AESL had restructured its debt recovery processes by strengthening the claims and finance departments to improve efficiency in collecting outstanding payments.

He disclosed that the company had already recovered a significant portion of outstanding debts owed for completed projects and remained committed to improving its financial sustainability.

The Managing Director explained that establishing proper contractual frameworks would help AESL protect its interests, maintain accountability, and ensure that professional services provided to clients are properly compensated.

AESL continues to implement reforms aimed at improving operational efficiency, strengthening financial management, and enhancing its ability to provide quality architectural, engineering, and consultancy services across Ghana.

title: AESL Clarifies Cost Differences in School Infrastructure Projects
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has explained that differences in the cost of school infrastructure projects are influenced by factors such as design specifications, materials used, project location, and facility requirements.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has provided clarification on concerns regarding the cost of school infrastructure projects, explaining that construction costs are determined by several technical factors including design, specifications, location, and the materials used.

The Acting Managing Director of AESL, Mr Louis Satchmo Atongo, explained that the first batch of basic school infrastructure projects awarded in 2003, which included six-unit classroom blocks and additional facilities, had a contract value of GH¢66,358.

He noted that the project package included a six-unit classroom block, office space, store, staff common room, library, and toilet facilities.

According to Mr Atongo, similar projects awarded in later years recorded higher costs due to changes in design requirements, expansion of classroom sizes to accommodate more pupils, and increases in the cost of construction materials.

He explained that making comparisons between projects requires an assessment of whether the buildings have the same designs, specifications, locations, and construction requirements, stressing that different projects cannot be compared without considering their unique characteristics.

AESL indicated that factors such as classroom dimensions, finishing standards, building components, and site conditions all contribute significantly to the final cost of a project.

Mr Atongo further explained that Senior High School classroom facilities generally attract higher costs because they often require larger spaces, improved finishes, ceiling works, and different building materials compared to basic school facilities.

He emphasised that a proper evaluation of construction costs must consider the complete scope of work rather than focusing only on the final contract figures.

The clarification was made following concerns raised about increases in the cost of school infrastructure projects and calls for greater transparency in public sector construction spending.

AESL reaffirmed its commitment to providing professional consultancy services and ensuring value for money in the delivery of educational infrastructure projects across Ghana.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/336388/aesl-clarifies-issues-on-the-cost-of-infrastructure-for-scho.html
tags: AESL, Architectural and Engineering Services Limited, School Infrastructure, Educational Projects, Construction Costs, Ghana Education Infrastructure, Building Projects
meta_title: AESL Explains Factors Affecting School Infrastructure Project Costs
meta_description: AESL has clarified that school construction costs are influenced by design, specifications, materials, location, and other technical requirements.

title: AESL Confirms Preservation of Historical Features in Old Parliament House Reconstruction
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has assured that the reconstruction works at the Old Parliament House would preserve the historical character and original design features of the facility.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has assured the public that the reconstruction of a section of the Old Parliament House would maintain the historical significance and architectural identity of the original structure.

The company, which provided consultancy services for the project, explained that the design of the building would not undergo major changes and that efforts would be made to preserve its original appearance.

The Managing Director of AESL, Mr Louis Satchmo Atongo, stated that available photographs and historical records of the building would guide the reconstruction process to ensure that the character of the facility remained intact.

He explained that the demolition was limited to a damaged section of the structure that had been affected by a fire incident in 2013, which left parts of the building unsafe for occupation.

According to AESL, assessments conducted on the affected portion showed that the structure had deteriorated significantly, making reconstruction from the foundation a more practical and cost-effective option than attempting extensive repairs.

Mr Atongo clarified that the entire Old Parliament House was not demolished, noting that significant portions of the historic building remained intact.

AESL provided both pre-contract and post-contract consultancy services for the project, including technical assessment, design guidance, and supervision of the reconstruction works.

The reconstruction project was aimed at restoring the damaged portion of the facility while ensuring that the historical value of the Old Parliament House was preserved for future generations.

title: AESL Supervised SHS Classroom Projects Face Delays Over Funding Challenges
excerpt: Some senior high school classroom projects supervised by Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) experienced delays as contractors raised concerns over inadequate funding support needed to complete the works.
content:
Some senior high school infrastructure projects being delivered under the supervision of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) experienced delays due to funding challenges affecting contractors working on the projects.

Contractors involved in the construction of additional six-unit classroom blocks for selected senior high schools appealed for government intervention to address financial difficulties affecting project completion.

One of the contractors, Alhaji Abubakar Alhassan, popularly known as Alhaji Ship Engine, explained that although his project had progressed significantly, including reaching the roofing stage, he had largely relied on personal resources to continue the work.

He indicated that the required financial support had not been provided to enable the project to proceed at the expected pace and called for assistance to ensure that the facility could be completed and made available to students.

The contractor noted that delays in completing educational infrastructure projects could affect schools that required additional classroom space to support growing student populations.

The concerns were raised during an inspection tour involving the Central Regional Minister, who visited completed and ongoing school infrastructure projects within the region.

During the visit, other classroom projects under AESL consultancy supervision were also observed at various stages of completion, with some contractors working to finish outstanding works.

AESL continues to provide professional consultancy services, including project supervision, technical monitoring, and quality assurance for educational infrastructure projects across Ghana.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/307190/aesl-shs-projects-at-standstill.html

title: AESL Reaffirms Commitment to Supporting Ghana’s Development Agenda
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has reaffirmed its commitment to supporting national development by providing professional consultancy services that deliver value, quality, and efficiency in infrastructure projects.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has reaffirmed its commitment to contributing to Ghana’s development agenda through the delivery of quality professional consultancy services for infrastructure projects.

The management of AESL stated that the organisation remains a reliable partner for government institutions, private sector organisations, and other stakeholders seeking expertise in architecture, engineering, and related consultancy services.

The Managing Director of AESL, Alhaji A.M. Mashood, made the commitment during a visit to the Upper East Region, where he engaged regional stakeholders and introduced the company’s regional leadership team.

He explained that AESL possesses a wide range of professional expertise under one institution, positioning the company to deliver projects efficiently while ensuring quality standards and value for money.

Alhaji Mashood encouraged regional coordinating councils, metropolitan, municipal and district assemblies, as well as development organisations, to continue engaging AESL for infrastructure planning, design, supervision, and consultancy services.

He emphasised that AESL’s technical capacity enables it to support projects from planning stages through construction supervision and completion, helping clients achieve sustainable development outcomes.

The Upper East Regional Minister, Mr Boniface Gambila, commended AESL for extending its professional services across the country and encouraged consultants to consider environmental conditions when developing infrastructure designs, especially in northern Ghana.

The Minister stressed the importance of incorporating climate-conscious approaches, including designs that respond to local environmental challenges.

During his engagement with AESL staff in the region, the Managing Director encouraged employees to remain dedicated and innovative, noting that increased competition in the consultancy sector requires continuous improvement and professionalism.

AESL reiterated that it would continue working towards strengthening Ghana’s infrastructure development through reliable, efficient, and professional consultancy services.

title: Contractors Demand Payment for AESL-Supervised School and Road Projects
excerpt: Contractors in the Upper East Region have appealed to government to settle outstanding payments for school and road projects awarded through Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL), warning that delays were affecting their businesses and financial obligations.
content:
Contractors in the Upper East Region have called on government to pay outstanding amounts owed for completed and ongoing infrastructure projects awarded through Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL).

The contractors, who worked on educational facilities and urban road projects, stated that delays in receiving payments had created financial difficulties and affected their ability to meet obligations to financial institutions and workers.

According to the contractors, the projects were awarded through AESL on behalf of the Ministry of Education, and many of them had relied on bank loans to pre-finance construction activities.

They explained that prolonged delays in payment had resulted in increased loan interest charges and placed pressure on their businesses and personal responsibilities.

The group appealed to the government and relevant institutions to intervene and ensure that contractors were paid for work completed.

They noted that other contractors who received similar projects through different funding arrangements had already received payments, creating concerns among those operating under the AESL-managed projects.

The contractors warned that failure to address the issue could compel them to pursue further actions, including demonstrations and legal measures, to recover outstanding payments.

They stressed that timely payment for completed projects was necessary to sustain local construction businesses and enable contractors to continue contributing to national infrastructure development.

title: AESL Staff Urged to Maintain Integrity and Professional Standards
excerpt: Staff of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) have been encouraged to uphold transparency, professionalism, and ethical standards while strengthening their competitiveness in the consultancy industry.
content:
Management and staff of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) have been urged to uphold high standards of integrity, transparency, and professionalism in the execution of their duties.

The Eastern Regional Minister, Mr Yaw Barimah, made the call during an end-of-year national management meeting of AESL held in Koforidua, where he encouraged the company’s workforce to remain committed to the vision and objectives of the organisation.

He advised staff to avoid actions that could damage the reputation of the company and cautioned against engaging in situations that could create conflicts of interest.

The Minister encouraged AESL to invest in continuous training and capacity development to enable its professionals to compete effectively in the open market based on quality service delivery rather than relying only on its position as a government consultancy institution.

He also called on AESL to utilise its training facilities at Weija, Accra, to improve the skills of its employees as well as local contractors, helping to raise construction standards across the country.

The Managing Director of AESL, Alhaji Mashud Ibn Musah, explained that the company’s nationwide presence gives it the ability to undertake projects in remote areas where other consultants may face operational challenges.

He noted that AESL had the technical capacity to prepare housing layouts and designs for various institutions, including regional coordinating councils and district assemblies.

Alhaji Musah further disclosed that AESL continued to support professional development through industrial training programmes for students from tertiary institutions across Ghana.

He added that the company was working towards improving project supervision through the establishment of zonal offices and strengthening its monitoring systems.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/91638/aesl-staff-advised-to-avoid-public-ridicule.html

title: Bank of Ghana Engages AESL for Value-for-Money Audit of New Headquarters Project
excerpt: The Bank of Ghana has engaged Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to conduct a value-for-money audit of its new headquarters project as part of efforts to promote transparency and accountability.
content:
The Bank of Ghana (BoG) has announced plans to engage Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to conduct a value-for-money audit of its new headquarters project in Accra.

The decision forms part of measures by the central bank to provide clarity on the cost and execution of the project following public discussions and concerns surrounding the financial investment in the facility.

Governor of the Bank of Ghana, Dr Johnson Asiama, disclosed that the audit would be undertaken after receiving approval from the newly constituted Board of Directors of the Bank.

He explained that the review would assess whether the cost of the project reflects prevailing market conditions and provide recommendations on improving cost efficiency and accountability.

The Governor stated that the audit would help address concerns surrounding the headquarters project and provide greater transparency regarding the expenditure involved in its construction.

The review comes amid public debate over the cost of the new Bank of Ghana headquarters, which was developed as part of efforts to provide modern infrastructure for the country’s central banking operations.

Dr Asiama reaffirmed the Bank’s commitment to prudent financial management, transparency, and maintaining public confidence through responsible decision-making.

He noted that accountability and sound financial practices remain central to the institution’s responsibility of promoting economic stability and growth.

Source: https://www.ghanaweb.com/GhanaHomePage/business/BoG-engages-AESL-for-value-for-money-audit-on-new-headquarters-1974195

title: AESL to Conduct Value-for-Money Audit on New Bank of Ghana Headquarters
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) is set to conduct a value-for-money audit of the new Bank of Ghana headquarters to provide clarity on project costs and ensure accountability.
content:
The Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) is expected to conduct a value-for-money audit of the new Bank of Ghana headquarters following approval from the Bank’s leadership.

The audit forms part of efforts by the Bank of Ghana to review the cost and implementation of the project while ensuring transparency and accountability in the use of public resources.

Governor of the Bank of Ghana, Dr Johnson Asiama, disclosed that arrangements had been made for AESL to undertake the assessment after receiving approval from the Bank’s Board.

According to the Governor, the review is expected to provide clarity on issues surrounding the cost of the building and help address public concerns regarding the project.

He explained that the total cost of the project increased after design adjustments were introduced, with significant payments already made towards construction, associated taxes, and other integrated systems.

The headquarters project includes additional infrastructure components such as communication systems, network infrastructure, security installations, and office furnishings designed to support the operations of a modern central bank.

The facility, located at Ridge in Accra, was developed to provide a modern working environment for the Bank of Ghana and strengthen its operational capacity.

The value-for-money audit by AESL will focus on assessing the efficiency of the project expenditure and determining whether the investment represents appropriate value.

title: AESL to Validate Ghana Institution of Surveyors’ Report on Saglemi Housing Project
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been tasked to validate the Ghana Institution of Surveyors’ report on the Saglemi Housing Project as part of efforts to assess the state and future of the stalled housing development.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been directed to validate a technical report prepared by the Ghana Institution of Surveyors (GhIS) on the Saglemi Housing Project.

The directive was issued by the Minister responsible for Housing following concerns surrounding the condition, completion status, and future use of the stalled housing project.

President of the Ghana Institution of Surveyors, Dr John Amaglo, explained that the request for AESL to review the report does not suggest that the findings of the institution are inaccurate or politically influenced.

He stated that as a professional body, the institution welcomed independent verification of its work by a competent organisation capable of conducting a technical assessment.

Dr Amaglo expressed confidence that an independent validation process would arrive at similar conclusions because the findings were based on physical assessments and professional evaluations conducted at the project site.

The Saglemi Housing Project has remained incomplete since 2016, leading to renewed discussions about how to complete and utilise the development to address Ghana’s housing deficit.

Dr Amaglo noted that the Ghana Institution of Surveyors conducted its assessment after experiencing challenges accessing some historical project documentation, requiring the team to undertake an independent evaluation of the available conditions on site.

He acknowledged that AESL, as the original consultant for the project, possessed relevant technical background and documentation, but emphasised the importance of ensuring that the validation process remained professional and independent.

The involvement of AESL is expected to provide additional technical insight into the condition of the project and guide decisions on the next steps for the development.

Source: https://www.myjoyonline.com/saglemi-housing-directive-for-validation-doesnt-mean-ghana-institution-of-surveyors-report-is-inappropriate-dr-amaglo/

title: AESL Completes Design Work for Appiatse Reconstruction Project Ahead of Construction
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has completed the foundation drawings and design phase for the reconstruction of Appiatse, paving the way for the commencement of construction works in the affected community.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has completed the foundation drawings and design work for the Appiatse Reconstruction Project, marking a major step towards the start of construction activities.

The project follows the devastating explosion that affected the Appiatse community near Bogoso in the Prestea-Huni-Valley Municipality of the Western Region. The reconstruction initiative is aimed at restoring the community through sustainable planning and improved infrastructure.

Deputy Minister of Lands and Natural Resources, Benito Owusu-Bio, who also serves as Chairman of the Appiatse Reconstruction Implementation Committee, disclosed that the design phase had been completed by AESL.

He explained that the committee would engage residents of Appiatse to gather their views and suggestions on the proposed designs before the final plans are approved for implementation.

Preparatory works at the project site included transferring the layout plan developed by the Land Use and Spatial Planning Authority (LUSPA) onto the land. Surveyors had already completed the demarcation of the core development area, which consists of 120 plots.

The initial phase of the reconstruction will focus on developing housing facilities within the designated core area, while other community facilities affected by the explosion will be rehabilitated or replaced as part of the broader redevelopment plan.

The government reiterated its commitment to rebuilding Appiatse into a modern, safe, and environmentally sustainable community while encouraging residents to support the reconstruction process.

Members of the implementation committee indicated that multiple contractors would be engaged to accelerate the project delivery timeline, with responsibilities distributed across different sections of the development area.

AESL is providing architectural and engineering consultancy services for the project, ensuring that the reconstruction meets professional standards and supports long-term community development.

Source: https://ghanaiantimes.com.gh/work-on-the-appiatse-reconstruction-project-is-scheduled-to-commence-by-middle-of-july-this-year/

title: AESL Urged to Adopt Innovative Strategies to Strengthen Business Growth
excerpt: The Ministry of Works and Housing has encouraged Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to embrace innovation, modern technologies, and new business approaches to improve its competitiveness and expand its operations.
content:
The Ministry of Works and Housing has urged Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to develop innovative strategies that will strengthen the company’s operations and improve its long-term sustainability.

Speaking at the company’s Annual General Meeting, the Minister for Works and Housing, Francis Asenso-Boakye, emphasised the need for AESL to adopt modern business practices and explore new opportunities beyond traditional government projects.

The Minister explained that advancements in technology, including Building Information Modelling (BIM) systems, provide opportunities for organisations in the built environment to improve efficiency, enhance project delivery, and attract new clients.

He encouraged AESL to move beyond dependence on government contracts and position itself as a competitive consultancy firm capable of serving both public and private sector clients.

The Minister acknowledged AESL’s reputation and extensive experience in architecture, engineering, and surveying, noting that the company’s nationwide presence gives it a strong advantage in the infrastructure sector.

During the meeting, the AESL Board Chairman, Professor Kwame Asamoah, welcomed the call for innovation and stated that the company would continue exploring ways to secure more consultancy opportunities.

He noted that challenges including delayed payments for completed projects had affected the company’s operations and appealed for stronger support to enable AESL to maximise its professional expertise.

The Managing Director of AESL, Isaac Agyei Marfo, indicated that the company had continued implementing measures aimed at improving revenue generation, strengthening debt recovery, and expanding opportunities within Ghana and other African markets.

AESL reaffirmed its commitment to providing professional consultancy services, maintaining high standards, and contributing to Ghana’s infrastructure development agenda.

Source: https://www.graphic.com.gh/news/general-news/make-aesl-viable-asenso-boakye.html

The Bank of Ghana has announced plans to engage Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to conduct a value-for-money audit on its new headquarters project in Accra.

The decision follows public discussions and concerns regarding the cost of the project, with the audit expected to provide an independent assessment of the project’s financial efficiency and value delivered.

Governor of the Bank of Ghana, Dr Johnson Asiama, disclosed that the proposed audit forms part of measures aimed at promoting transparency, accountability, and responsible management of public resources.

According to the Governor, the newly constituted Bank of Ghana Board would review key legacy matters, including the headquarters project, before authorising AESL to carry out the assessment.

The audit by AESL will examine whether the cost of the project reflects prevailing market conditions and determine whether resources were utilised efficiently during the execution of the development.

Architectural and Engineering Services Limited, a state-owned consultancy firm with expertise in architecture, engineering, quantity surveying, and project supervision, has been recognised for its technical capacity in evaluating major infrastructure projects.

The Bank of Ghana stated that the exercise demonstrates its commitment to strengthening public confidence through transparency, sound decision-making, and prudent financial management.

The central bank further reaffirmed that maintaining accountability in major infrastructure investments remains essential to supporting Ghana’s economic development goals.

title: AESL Documents Confirm Cost of Vice President’s Official Residence Project
excerpt: Documents from Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) indicated the estimated cost of the official residence project for Ghana’s Vice Presidents, providing details on construction expenses and payments made.
content:
Documents from Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) have provided details on the cost of the official residence project for Ghana’s Vice Presidents, a project that attracted public discussion over its financial value.

The AESL document, titled consultancy services for the administration and supervision of construction works for the official residence of the Vice Presidents of the Republic of Ghana, outlined the estimated project cost and related construction details.

According to the document, the total cost of the project included various components such as construction works, external development, security features, electrical works, landscaping, and other specialised installations.

The records indicated that payments had already been made towards the project, while an outstanding balance remained for the completion of the works.

AESL, as the state institution responsible for providing architectural and engineering consultancy services, was involved in the costing and supervision aspects of the project.

The cost of the residence became a subject of public debate following comments from political actors regarding the amount allocated for the development.

Supporters of the project argued that the official residence was an important national facility, while critics questioned whether the expenditure represented value for money.

The AESL documentation contributed technical information to the discussion by outlining the scope, specifications, and financial requirements of the project.

AESL continues to provide professional consultancy services for major government infrastructure projects, including design, supervision, and technical assessments.

title: AESL Provides Consultancy Services for Rehabilitation of COCOBOD Building in Sunyani
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) is providing consultancy services for the rehabilitation of the six-storey COCOBOD building in Sunyani to improve its facilities and extend its usability.
content:
Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has been engaged as consultants for the rehabilitation of the six-storey COCOBOD building in Sunyani.

The rehabilitation project, awarded to Premier Shelter Limited, an Accra-based civil engineering and construction firm, was expected to improve the condition and functionality of the building.

Speaking during a meeting with tenants of the building, the Brong Ahafo Regional Director of AESL, Mr Emmanuel Aggor, explained that the project involved replacing deteriorated internal and external fittings.

The rehabilitation works included changing doors and windows, improving wall finishes, replacing ceilings, upgrading toilet facilities, and providing ramps to make the building more accessible for persons with disabilities.

Mr Aggor added that existing services such as electrical installations, fire alarm systems, and telephone facilities would be repaired and improved as part of the project.

The project also included improvements to plumbing services, including the provision of a new cesspit tank, as well as redesigning parking areas around the building.

The COCOBOD building, popularly known as Cocoa House, is one of the notable structures in the Sunyani area and had served as an important office facility for many years.

AESL’s involvement in the project reflects its role in providing professional architectural, engineering, and project supervision services for public infrastructure developments across Ghana.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/199907/cocobod-building-in-sunyani-under-rehabilitation.html

title: Ghana Is Running a Spoil System - Experts Warn Against Compromising Professional Standards
excerpt: Executive Director of Safety Ghana, Nana Annor Amihere, has criticised professionals at state institutions for compromising standards and allowing poor-quality works, raising concerns about the supervision of major public projects including the Job 600 renovation.
content:
Executive Director of Safety Ghana, Nana Annor Amihere, has raised concerns about the declining professional standards within some state institutions, accusing experts of compromising their responsibilities to satisfy political authorities.

His comments followed concerns surrounding the $25 million renovation of the Job 600 building, a project intended to provide offices for Members of Parliament, where reports indicated the use of inferior and substandard materials.

The Chinese contractor responsible for the renovation, China State Hualong Construction Company, was ordered to remove fittings and structures considered unsuitable for the facility.

During an inspection of the building by Parliament’s Committee on Works and Housing, the Chief Resident Engineer, Ekow Bentsi-Echill, admitted that some materials used in the renovation were not suitable.

Nana Annor Amihere questioned why professionals responsible for supervising the project allowed such materials to be used, arguing that engineers should uphold professional standards regardless of circumstances.

He also questioned the role of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL), which provides professional consultancy and advisory services to government on major infrastructure projects.

According to him, institutions responsible for protecting public interest must remain independent and ensure that quality standards are maintained in government projects.

He further recommended that an independent geotechnical and safety inspection should be carried out on the Job 600 building to ensure the facility is safe for occupation.

Nana Annor Amihere stated that lessons from previous building disasters should encourage authorities to conduct proper inspections and enforce strict safety standards on public infrastructure.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/455395/ghana-is-running-a-spoil-system-exe-dir-of-safety-ghan.html

meta_title: Experts Raise Concerns Over Professional Standards in Public Projects

title: Ghana and Togo Agree to Revive Joint Water Supply Project
excerpt: Ghana and Togo have agreed to revive the Sogakope-Lome Transboundary Water Supply Project aimed at providing water from the Volta River to communities in both countries.
content:
Ghana and Togo have agreed to implement the Sogakope-Lome Transboundary Water Supply Project, a major initiative aimed at supplying water from the Volta River in Ghana to Lome, the capital city of Togo.

The project, which has been under discussion since the 1970s, will involve the extraction of water from the Volta River at Sogakope in Ghana and is expected to benefit communities along the Volta Lake corridor.

The African Development Bank (AfDB) expressed interest in supporting the preparation and implementation phases of the project, including updating feasibility studies, environmental and social impact assessments, and preparing tender documents.

An agreement to advance the project was signed by Ghana’s Minister of Water Resources, Works and Housing, Mr E.T. Mensah, on behalf of the Ghanaian government, while the Togolese Minister of Sanitation and Rural Water, Mr Zakari Nandja, represented the Togolese government.

Speaking after the signing ceremony, both ministers highlighted the importance of the project in strengthening cooperation between Ghana and Togo and improving access to safe water for citizens.

Mr E.T. Mensah explained that the project demonstrated the commitment of both countries to regional integration and improving public health, hygiene, and living standards within the project areas.

He recalled that the project originated from feasibility studies conducted by the Water Division of the then Architectural and Engineering Services Corporation, now Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL).

The ministers expressed hope that once funding was secured, the project would be completed within two years and provide lasting benefits to communities in Ghana and Togo.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/395711/ghana-togo-agree-to-revive-joint-water-project.html

title: Akufo-Addo Consoles Victims of Kantamanto Fire Disaster
excerpt: Nana Addo Dankwa Akufo-Addo has expressed sympathy to victims of the Kantamanto fire disaster and called for improved planning and safety measures in Ghana’s markets.
content:
Nana Addo Dankwa Akufo-Addo, the 2012 presidential candidate of the New Patriotic Party, has expressed his deepest condolences to victims of the Kantamanto market fire disaster.

Describing the incident as a sad moment in Ghana’s history, he called on relevant authorities to implement effective measures to prevent similar disasters from occurring in the future.

Nana Addo expressed concern about the increasing number of fire outbreaks in markets across the country and called for a standardised strategic approach to market design and construction.

He emphasised that markets play an important role in Ghana’s economy and support the livelihoods of thousands of families, making it necessary to develop safer and more resilient market structures.

He proposed that professional institutions including the Ghana Institution of Engineers, Ghana Institution of Architects, Architectural Engineering Services Limited (AESL), and the Ghana National Fire Service should collaborate in designing modern market facilities.

According to him, innovative planning and improved construction standards were needed to reduce the risk of future fires and protect businesses, property, and lives.

Nana Addo also prayed for the victims and encouraged authorities to provide the necessary support to affected traders as they recover from their losses.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/462450/akufo-addo-consoles-victims-of-kantamanto-fire-disaster.html

title: China To Help Build New Foreign Ministry Office Complex
excerpt: The Chinese government agreed to support Ghana in constructing a modern office complex for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, with AESL providing technical input on the project design.
content:
The Chinese government has agreed to construct a modern office complex for Ghana’s Ministry of Foreign Affairs following discussions between the two countries.

The proposed office complex was planned for construction at the former International Students Hostel site near Airport Junction in Accra and was expected to be completed within two years.

The Minister of Foreign Affairs, Alhaji Mohammed Mumuni, announced the development after the Chinese Embassy donated office equipment to support the ministry following a fire that destroyed its previous building.

The Chinese Ambassador to Ghana confirmed that both governments had reached an agreement for the construction of the new office facility and assured that processes would be accelerated to begin work.

The Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) reviewed the initial prototype design submitted by the Chinese government and provided technical recommendations based on Ghana’s requirements.

The input from AESL was intended to improve the planning and structure of the proposed office complex before final approval and implementation.

Alhaji Mumuni explained that the project formed part of Ghana’s long-term plan to provide a suitable working environment for the Ministry of Foreign Affairs.

He expressed appreciation to the Chinese government for its support and described the assistance as a reflection of the strong relationship between Ghana and China.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/247068/china-to-help-build-new-foreign-ministry.html

title: Begoro Presbyterian SHS Science Resource Centre Abandoned Affecting Science Education
excerpt: The abandonment of the rehabilitation project for the science resource centre at Begoro Presbyterian Senior High School has affected science teaching and learning, with AESL identified as the supervising consultant.
content:
Students of Begoro Presbyterian Senior High School and other schools within the surrounding communities have faced challenges in science education due to the abandonment of a rehabilitation project for the school’s science resource centre.

The rehabilitation contract was awarded to Messrs MOFERG Company Limited in November 2010 at a cost of GH¢130,152.90, with completion expected within four months.

However, the contractor abandoned the project after carrying out some initial works, leaving the facility incomplete and affecting practical science lessons.

The Headmaster of the school explained that the absence of a functional laboratory had made teaching and learning difficult, forcing teachers and students to rely on a makeshift laboratory created from a classroom.

The situation contributed to a decline in the number and performance of science students, as many students transferred to other schools due to the lack of proper facilities.

Teachers at the school expressed concern that the lack of a science resource centre had affected students’ ability to undertake practical work and compete academically.

The rehabilitation project was supervised by the Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL), which was expected to provide technical oversight and monitor the progress of the work.

The District Chief Executive pledged to follow up with AESL and the Ministry of Education to determine the cause of the abandonment and seek a solution to restore the facility.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/809139/begoro-presbyterian-shs-science-resource-centre-abandoned-s.html

title: Pozzolana Factory Closes Down For Lack Of Patronage
excerpt: Pozzolana Ghana Limited’s factory at Gomoa Mprumem has been shut down after five months of commercial production due to low patronage of pozzolana cement, resulting in workers being laid off and thousands of bags remaining unsold.
content:
The Pozzolana Ghana Limited factory at Gomoa Mprumem in the Central Region has been shut down five months after commencing commercial production due to poor patronage of pozzolana cement in Ghana.

Pozzolana, produced from clay and palm kernel shells, is a cheaper alternative building material discovered by the Building and Road Research Institute (BRRI) of the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) after more than 30 years of research.

The product was expected to reduce construction costs while improving the quality and durability of concrete and mortar. It enhances workability, reduces heat of hydration, bleeding and segregation of concrete, and improves resistance against sulphate attacks and alkali aggregate reactions.

Despite its benefits, the product struggled to gain market acceptance. The factory, which began full operations in March, was producing about 2,000 bags daily with a workforce of 86 employees and had the capacity to expand production to 5,000 bags per day.

The Managing Director of Pozzolana Ghana Limited, Mr Charles Hegbor, explained that the company was forced to lay off workers because sales had declined significantly.

He stated that the company had more than 40,000 bags of unsold products in its warehouse and could no longer afford to pay workers due to poor sales.

The shutdown also affected women in the Gomoa area who supplied palm kernel shells used in producing the material.

The Director of the Building and Road Research Institute (BRRI), Mr Eugene Atiemo, expressed concern about the lack of support for a locally developed innovation and called for deliberate government policies to promote locally manufactured building materials.

However, the Ministry of Water Resources, Works and Housing and Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) attributed the poor patronage partly to inadequate marketing by the company.

The Director of Housing at the Ministry, Alhaji Yahya Yakubu, said many Ghanaians were unaware of the existence of pozzolana and encouraged stronger marketing strategies to increase adoption, especially within the private construction sector.

The Acting Managing Director of AESL, Mr Louis Satchmo Atongo, advised the company to engage accredited agents across the regions and work with AESL regional directors to encourage contractors to use pozzolana in their projects.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/345833/pozzolana-factory-closes-down-for-lack-of-patronage.html
tags: AESL, Architectural and Engineering Services Limited, Pozzolana Ghana Limited, Building Materials, Construction Industry, BRRI, Ghana Housing Development
meta_title: Pozzolana Factory Shutdown Highlights Challenges Facing Local Building Materials
meta_description: Pozzolana Ghana Limited has shut down its factory due to poor patronage of pozzolana cement, with AESL and government officials calling for improved marketing and adoption of local building materials.

title: Committee Report on Bawku Schools Disturbances Released After Investigation
excerpt: A committee set up to investigate disturbances between Bawku Technical Institute and Bawku Senior High School has released its findings, identifying causes of the conflict, assessing damages, and recommending measures to prevent future incidents.
content:
A committee established to investigate disturbances between Bawku Technical Institute (BTI) and Bawku Senior High School (BSHS) has submitted its report on the events that led to injuries and destruction of property at both institutions.

The committee, chaired by Mr Luke Abugri, Principal of Gbewaa Teacher Training College, was tasked to determine the causes of the disturbances, identify those responsible, assess damages, and recommend measures to promote lasting peace between the two schools.

The investigation involved interviews with students, school authorities, teachers, security personnel, and physical inspections of affected areas. The committee also relied on reports from various institutions, including Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL).

The committee established that the immediate cause of the conflict was a fight between two students of Bawku Technical Institute on the campus of Bawku Senior High School. Attempts by students and staff to intervene failed, leading to retaliatory actions between students of both institutions.

The findings revealed that students from both schools were involved in the disturbances, with six students from BTI identified as initiators and another six from BSHS involved in retaliatory actions.

According to the AESL assessment, the value of properties destroyed at Bawku Technical Institute was estimated at GH¢8,843, while damage at Bawku Senior High School amounted to GH¢1,078.98, bringing the total cost of destruction to GH¢10,021.98.

The committee made sixteen recommendations, fourteen of which were accepted by the Regional Coordinating Council. These included improved supervision, adherence to school regulations, and stronger cooperation with security agencies.

Although the committee recommended the dismissal of five BTI students, the Regional Minister exercised his prerogative of mercy and changed the punishment to a one-year suspension after appeals from journalists and stakeholders.

The affected students included Maurice Kaguah, Forster Ayamga, Edward Kodabalogo, Kadiri Osman, and Rashid Bukari.

The Regional Minister commended the committee for its professional approach and encouraged both schools to implement the recommendations to ensure peaceful coexistence.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/news/221530/committee-report-on-bawku-schools-disturbances-out-five-stu.html

title: Asantehene Builds 600-Bed Girls’ Dormitory for Sefwi Wiawso Senior High School
excerpt: The Asantehene, Otumfuo Osei Tutu II, through the Otumfuo Osei Tutu II Foundation, has handed over a newly constructed three-storey girls’ dormitory with a 600-bed capacity to Sefwi Wiawso Senior High School, with AESL serving as the consulting firm.
content:
The Asantehene, Otumfuo Osei Tutu II, through the Otumfuo Osei Tutu II Foundation, has officially handed over a newly constructed three-storey girls’ dormitory to Sefwi Wiawso Senior High School in the Western North Region.

The modern dormitory has a capacity of 600 beds and includes ten spacious dormitory rooms with lavatories on each floor, open washrooms, a washing and ironing area, a common room, a storeroom, an infirmary, and a two-bedroom apartment for the housemistress.

The project was executed by Attachy Construction Limited, with Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) providing consultancy services for the development.

Representing the Asantehene at the handing-over ceremony was the Otumfuo Hiahene and Board Chairman of the Otumfuo Osei Tutu II Foundation, Nana Prof. Oheneba Boachie-Adjei Woahene II.

He officially presented the keys of the completed facility to traditional leaders of the area, who subsequently handed them over to the school authorities.

The project reflects Otumfuo Osei Tutu II’s commitment to improving access to quality education and creating safe, inclusive, and conducive learning environments for students across Ghana.

The new facility is expected to improve accommodation conditions for female students and support academic excellence at Sefwi Wiawso Senior High School.

title: Tension in Tema as Occupants Oppose Demolition of Kaiser Flats Over Safety Risks
excerpt: Residents of Kaiser Flats in Tema Community 4 are resisting plans to demolish six blocks of the housing complex after authorities declared the structures unsafe, following structural assessments including findings from AESL.
content:
Residents of Kaiser Flats in Tema Community 4 have opposed plans to demolish six blocks of the historic housing complex, insisting that the buildings remain suitable for occupation despite safety concerns raised by authorities.

The housing complex, which is associated with Ghana’s post-independence housing development programme, has become the centre of safety discussions due to visible signs of deterioration, including cracks and weakened sections.

The Greater Accra Regional office of the National Disaster Management Organisation (NADMO) warned that some of the structures pose significant risks to occupants and could collapse if urgent measures are not taken.

Authorities indicated that a structural integrity assessment conducted by Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) found that six blocks had failed safety tests and were no longer fit for human habitation.

Officials explained that the decision was not targeted at the entire housing complex, as other blocks remained structurally sound and only required maintenance and renovation.

Residents, however, disagreed with the findings and argued that the buildings were constructed with reinforced concrete and remained strong. Some occupants questioned why concerns about the structures were not raised earlier when the properties were sold to them.

The planned demolition forms part of broader efforts by NADMO to address unsafe buildings and prevent future disasters following several building collapse incidents across the country.

Authorities have maintained that the intervention is aimed at protecting lives and ensuring that residents do not continue living in structures that pose serious safety risks.

Source: https://www.myjoyonline.com/tension-in-tema-as-occupants-oppose-demolition-of-kaiser-flats-over-safety-risks/

title: Saglemi Housing Project: Lack of Electricity and Water Supply Stalling Habitation
excerpt: The Ghana Institution of Surveyors has identified the lack of electricity and water supply as major challenges preventing residents from occupying completed portions of the Saglemi Housing Project, with AESL tasked to validate the assessment report.
content:
The Ghana Institution of Surveyors (GhIS) has identified the absence of essential utilities such as electricity and water supply as key reasons why citizens have been unable to occupy completed sections of the Saglemi Housing Project.

The concerns follow discussions surrounding the housing project after a video advocating for mass occupation of the facility circulated publicly.

The Minister for Works and Housing directed the Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) to validate the report submitted by the Ghana Institution of Surveyors on the state of the project and provide professional advice.

President of the Ghana Institution of Surveyors, Dr John Amaglo, explained that the validation exercise did not suggest that their report was inaccurate or influenced by political considerations.

According to him, connecting basic services including electricity and water would make the completed housing units suitable for occupation.

He stated that once the necessary utilities were extended to the project site, completed houses could be handed over and occupied by citizens.

Dr Amaglo further urged government to accelerate efforts to complete viable portions of the project, noting that this would contribute towards reducing Ghana’s housing deficit.

He emphasised that completing existing housing projects would provide much-needed accommodation for citizens rather than allowing completed structures to remain unused.

The involvement of AESL in validating the assessment highlights the importance of professional review and technical guidance in ensuring the successful completion and utilisation of major public infrastructure projects.

Source: https://www.myjoyonline.com/saglemi-housing-project-lack-of-electricity-water-supply-stalling-habitation-ghana-institution-of-surveyors/

title: Sports Ministry To Spend Over $3 Million To Give Essipong Stadium A Facelift
excerpt: The Ministry of Youth, Sports and Culture has announced a $3 million renovation project for Essipong Stadium, with AESL providing supervision services for the rehabilitation works.
content:
The Ministry of Youth, Sports and Culture has announced plans to spend over US$3 million on the first phase of renovation works at the Essipong Stadium in Sekondi.

The decision follows an inspection of the facility by the Minister for Youth, Sports and Culture, Hon. Isaac Kwame Asiamah, and other government officials, who assessed the poor condition of the stadium.

The Minister explained that the renovation was aimed at restoring the facility to an acceptable standard while ensuring a proper maintenance culture after completion.

He stated that maintenance manuals would be developed for renovated stadia to guide future management and prevent similar deterioration.

The first phase of the project will be executed by CISDAN Ltd, with Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) serving as the supervising consultants.

The rehabilitation works will include the removal and replacement of defective roofing sheets, repairs to damaged roof structural steel members, and replacement of bolts and other weakened components.

Additional works will involve corrosion protection through sandblasting and application of protective coating systems to preserve the stadium’s metal structures.

The project will also include waterproofing of leaking areas, repairs to lifts, fire-fighting systems, air-conditioning units, office renovations, and improvements to plumbing facilities.

The contractors are expected to complete the renovation works within 12 months, after which the stadium will be available for improved sporting activities and events.

Source: https://www.modernghana.com/sports/1000004/sports-ministry-to-spend-over-3-million-to-give.html

title: GNA CEO and Technical Teams Inspect Ongoing Africa Development Bank Funded Project in Tamale
excerpt: Technical teams from AESL and the Social Investment Fund have inspected renovation works at the Ghana News Agency office block in Tamale, a project funded by the African Development Bank.
content:
The Chief Executive Officer of the Ghana News Agency (GNA), Mr Albert Kofi Owusu, has led technical teams from Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) and the Social Investment Fund (SIF) to inspect ongoing renovation works at the GNA office block in Tamale.

The renovation project is funded by the African Development Bank under the Post Covid-19 Skills Development and Productivity Enhancement Project (PSDPEP), with technical support from SIF and AESL.

The project forms part of a broader $24 million African Development Bank initiative aimed at supporting Ghana’s economic recovery and improving productivity after the COVID-19 pandemic.

The inspection team expressed satisfaction with the pace and quality of work being delivered by the contractor, noting that progress was ahead of schedule.

The renovation works include replacing louvre blades with sliding windows, complete reroofing of the structure, changing floor finishes from PVC to ceramic tiles, rewiring electrical systems, and improving water, sewerage, and plumbing facilities.

Additional structural improvements such as fencing and the development of storage facilities to support income generation are also being considered under the project.

The GNA office building, which has existed for about 50 years without major renovation, is expected to receive a significant facelift to improve working conditions and operational efficiency.

Mr Isaac S. Agyei-Mensah, Zonal Consultant at AESL in Tamale, commended the contractor for maintaining quality standards and making significant progress on the project.

He encouraged the contractor to address minor concerns raised during the inspection and continue adhering to the required project specifications.

title: AESL to Assist MMDAs with Affordable Housing Projects
excerpt: Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL) has expressed readiness to collaborate with Metropolitan, Municipal and District Assemblies to support affordable housing development across Ghana.
content:
The Managing Director of Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL), Mr Louis Satchmo Attia Atongo, has expressed the company’s readiness to collaborate with Metropolitan, Municipal and District Assemblies (MMDAs) to implement affordable housing projects across the country.

He explained that government’s commitment to providing affordable housing for workers required strong partnerships with local authorities and technical institutions.

Mr Atongo advised Regional Coordinating Councils and district assemblies to work together to secure land banks that could be developed for housing purposes.

He stated that AESL, with its extensive technical expertise, could assist in preparing layout schemes, house designs, and professional solutions required for successful housing development.

Speaking at an end-of-year management meeting of AESL staff in Bolgatanga, he identified inadequate construction skills and weak contract administration practices as some of the major challenges affecting the quality of buildings in Ghana.

He noted that poor workmanship among some rural artisans contributed to the deterioration of buildings and emphasised the need to promote improved construction methods and skills development.

To address these challenges, AESL would continue organising workshops and seminars for district assemblies, Regional Coordinating Councils, and contractors to improve their capacity in contract administration and construction practices.

Mr Atongo also disclosed that AESL designed government’s community day senior high schools and was supervising the construction of 19 such schools across several regions in Ghana.

He highlighted challenges facing the company, including delays in payment for services rendered and difficulties supervising projects located in remote areas with challenging terrain.

The Upper East Regional Minister, Mr Albert Abongo, commended AESL for its role in designing community day senior high schools and encouraged consultants to ensure quality delivery of projects.

Source: https://www.graphic.com.gh/news/general-news/aesl-to-assist-mmdas-with-affordable-housing-projects.html

title: NER Minister Inspects Ongoing RCC Building Projects
excerpt: The North East Regional Minister has inspected ongoing construction works on the Regional Coordinating Council office complex and staff bungalows in Nalerigu, with AESL providing technical supervision.
content:
The North East Regional Minister, Mr Solomon Namlit Boar, has inspected ongoing construction works on the Regional Coordinating Council (RCC) office complex at Nalerigu, the capital of the North East Region.

The Regional Administration currently operates from a rented facility, making the completion of the new RCC office complex an important step towards strengthening administrative operations in the region.

The Minister was accompanied by the Municipal Chief Executive for Gambaga, Mr Danladi Abdul-Nashir, officials of the RCC, and representatives from Architectural and Engineering Services Limited (AESL).

The delegation also inspected ongoing construction works on staff bungalows for officials of the Ghana Health Service at Gambaga.

After inspecting the RCC facility, which includes attached staff accommodation, the Minister expressed satisfaction with the progress of work and confidence in the quality of construction.

He indicated that the contractor had assured the team that work would continue at an accelerated pace to ensure completion within the scheduled period.

The AESL team also confirmed that the project was progressing well, with major foundation works completed and activities focused on fixtures and finishing stages.

Mr Gordon Tanga, Northern Regional Consultant of AESL, explained that the project formed part of a wider development programme introduced by the Ministry of Regional Reorganisation and Development for newly created regions.

He stated that ten projects were awarded under the programme, with the RCC office complex being among them.

The ground floor of the RCC building includes office spaces, store rooms, a conference hall, and a restaurant, while the upper floor contains additional offices and meeting rooms.

Mr Tanga disclosed that the project was handed over to the contractor in November and was expected to be completed within 12 calendar months.

The contract value was estimated at about GH¢17.6 million, with AESL providing consultancy and supervision services to ensure quality delivery.

Source: https://www.newsghana.com.gh/ner-minister-inspects-ongoing-rcc-building-projects/
